title: Technology Project Governance
verbatim: A financial institution must establish appropriate governance requirements commensurate with the risk and complexity of technology projects undertaken. This shall include project oversight roles and responsibilities, authority and reporting structures, and risk assessments throughout the project life cycle.
translation: A financial institution must put in place governance arrangements that match the risk and complexity of its technology projects. These arrangements must cover project oversight roles and responsibilities, authority and reporting structures, and risk assessments conducted throughout the entire project life cycle.
evidence: Technology Project Governance Policy and Framework
Project Oversight Roles and Responsibilities Register
IT Steering Committee and Project Governance Meeting Records
Technology Project Risk Assessment Records
Project Authority and Reporting Structure Documentation

artifacts.name: IT Project Governance Framework
artifacts.description: Framework defining project governance structures, roles, and approval gates

artifacts.name: Project Approval Records
artifacts.description: Records of project approvals and stage-gate sign-offs

title: Technology Project Risk Assessment
translation: Risk assessments for technology projects must identify and address key risks that could threaten successful implementation and risks that project failure could have on the institution's broader operational capabilities. At a minimum, assessments must cover resource adequacy and vendor competency, system complexity and integration risks, security controls throughout the project life cycle, user requirement comprehensiveness, testing robustness, deployment and fallback strategies, and disaster recovery readiness after implementation.
evidence: Technology Project Risk Assessment Register
Vendor and Resource Competency Assessment
System Complexity and Integration Risk Assessment
Project Security Control Review Records
User Requirements Specification and Change Control Records

artifacts.name: Project Risk Assessment Methodology
artifacts.description: Standard methodology and template for assessing technology project risks

artifacts.name: Project Risk Assessment Reports
artifacts.description: Independent risk assessment reports for major technology projects

artifacts.name: Interdependency Analysis
artifacts.description: Analysis of project interdependencies with existing systems

title: Board and Senior Management Project Reporting
verbatim: The board and senior management must receive and review timely reports on the management of these risks on an ongoing basis throughout the implementation of significant projects.
translation: The board and senior management must receive and review timely reports on how risks in significant technology projects are being managed, and this reporting must continue on an ongoing basis throughout the entire implementation period.
evidence: Significant Technology Project Classification Policy
Board and Senior Management Technology Project Risk Reports
Board and Senior Management Meeting Minutes — Technology Project Risk Agenda Items
Project Risk Reporting Calendar and Reporting Cadence Log
Technology Project Risk Register for Significant Projects

artifacts.name: Board Technology Risk Reports
artifacts.description: Mandatory document for Board Technology Risk Reports

artifacts.name: Project Status Reports
artifacts.description: Mandatory document for Project Status Reports

title: Enterprise Technology Architecture Framework
translation: A financial institution must establish a framework to guide the design, planning, implementation, and governance of its enterprise technology architecture. The framework must provide a comprehensive view of the institution's technology, describe infrastructure and system interdependencies, map technology to business functions and data to support impact analysis, define network and security governance principles, and outline longer-term priorities for the architecture's evolution.
evidence: Enterprise Technology Architecture Framework Policy Document
Baseline Architecture Inventory and Component Register
Technology Infrastructure and System Interdependency Maps
Business-Technology Mapping Matrix
Architecture Principles, Network Governance Standards, and IT Security Policy Suite

artifacts.name: Enterprise Technology Architecture Framework
artifacts.description: Mandatory document for Enterprise Technology Architecture Framework

artifacts.name: Architecture Review Board Minutes
artifacts.description: Mandatory document for Architecture Review Board Minutes

title: System Development Life Cycle Methodology
verbatim:
A financial institution must adopt a methodology for an effective and secure implementation of IT systems. Key phases of System Development Life Cycle (SDLC) shall include requirement, design, development, testing, deployment, change management, maintenance and decommissioning, and integrate with-
(a) enterprise architecture to ensure successful execution of business strategy;
(b) risk management policies and practices to achieve business objectives; and
(c) security principles and requirements to ensure confidentiality, integrity, and availability of customer and counterparty information.
translation: A financial institution must adopt a methodology for effective and secure IT system implementation covering the key SDLC phases: requirements, design, development, testing, deployment, change management, maintenance, and decommissioning. The methodology must integrate with enterprise architecture, risk management policies, and security principles to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of customer and counterparty information.
evidence: SDLC Methodology Policy and Framework Document
SDLC Phase-Gate Artefacts and Project Sign-Off Records
Enterprise Architecture Integration Evidence
Security Integration Records Across SDLC Phases
Risk Management Integration in SDLC

artifacts.name: SDLC Framework Document
artifacts.description: Formal SDLC framework with security integrated at every phase

artifacts.name: Quality Gate Records
artifacts.description: Records of quality gate assessments and sign-offs for projects

artifacts.name: Security Checkpoint Evidence
artifacts.description: Evidence of security reviews conducted at each SDLC phase

title: Rapid Development Security Requirements
verbatim: A financial institution must meet enterprise security, governance and compliance requirements when using rapid system development methodology. Given the dynamic environment can increase likelihood of errors, a financial institution shall automate the IT security compliance review to prevent unauthorised access as well as the discovery and testing of security vulnerabilities to ensure secure release of new IT services.
translation: When using rapid system development methodology, a financial institution must still meet enterprise security, governance, and compliance requirements. Because the dynamic environment increases the likelihood of errors, the institution must automate IT security compliance reviews and the discovery and testing of security vulnerabilities to ensure new IT services are released securely.
evidence: Rapid Development Security Policy and Framework
CI/CD Pipeline Security Automation Configuration Records
Automated Security Scan Results and Remediation Records
Secure Release Gate and Deployment Approval Records
Access Control and Privileged Access Records for Development Environments

artifacts.name: Security Requirements Specification
artifacts.description: Mandatory document for Security Requirements Specification

artifacts.name: Requirements Traceability Matrix
artifacts.description: Matrix tracing security requirements through development phases to validation

artifacts.name: Requirements Validation Records
artifacts.description: Records demonstrating validation of security requirements prior to deployment

title: Production Environment Segregation
verbatim: A financial institution shall physically segregate the production environment from the development and testing environment to mitigate the risk of unauthorised changes to the production systems. Where a financial institution is relying on a cloud environment, the financial institution shall ensure that these environments are not running on the same virtual host.
translation: A financial institution must physically segregate the production environment from development and testing environments to prevent unauthorised changes to production systems. Where the institution uses a cloud environment, it must ensure that production, development, and testing environments do not run on the same virtual host.
evidence: Environment Segregation Architecture Document
Access Control Matrix — Environment-Level Separation
Cloud Virtual Host Isolation Evidence
Network Segmentation and Firewall Rule Evidence
Change Management Records Demonstrating Environment Gate Controls

artifacts.name: Environment Separation Policy
artifacts.description: Policy defining separation requirements between development, test, and production environments

artifacts.name: Environment Architecture Diagram
artifacts.description: Architecture diagram showing separation between environments

title: System Testing Methodology
verbatim: A financial institution must establish a sound methodology for a rigorous system testing prior to deployment. The testing shall ensure that the system meets user requirements and performs robustly. Where sensitive test data is used, the financial institution must ensure proper authorisation procedures and adequate measures to prevent their unauthorised disclosure are in place.
translation: A financial institution must establish a sound and rigorous testing methodology before any system is deployed, ensuring the system meets user requirements and performs robustly. Where sensitive test data is used, the institution must have proper authorisation procedures and adequate measures in place to prevent its unauthorised disclosure.
evidence: System Testing Policy and Methodology Framework
Test Plans and Test Case Documentation
Test Execution Results and Defect Management Records
User Acceptance Testing (UAT) Sign-off Records
Sensitive Test Data Authorisation and Handling Procedures

artifacts.name: Test Summary Reports
artifacts.description: Summary reports of testing outcomes and approval decisions

title: Scope of System Testing
verbatim: The scope of system testing referred to in paragraph 10.8 may include unit testing, integration testing, user acceptance testing, application security testing, stress and load testing, regression, exception and negative testing, where applicable.
translation: The scope of system testing referred to in paragraph 10.8 may include unit testing, integration testing, user acceptance testing, application security testing, stress and load testing, regression testing, exception testing, and negative testing, as applicable to the system being tested.
evidence: System Testing Strategy and Scope Document
Unit and Integration Test Results

title: Source Code Review for Critical Systems
verbatim: A financial institution must ensure any changes to the source code of critical systems are subject to adequate source code reviews to ensure code is secure and developed in line with recognised coding practices prior to introducing any system changes.
translation: A financial institution must ensure that any changes to the source code of critical systems are subject to adequate source code reviews before those changes are introduced. The review must confirm that the code is secure and has been developed in line with recognised coding practices.
evidence: Source Code Review Policy and Procedure
Critical Systems Inventory
Source Code Review Records per Change
Secure Coding Standards and Review Checklist
Code Review Finding Remediation and Closure Records

artifacts.description: Mandatory procedure for peer code reviews and SAST scanning

artifacts.name: SAST Scan Results
artifacts.description: Static analysis scan results for application releases

title: Independent Review of System Changes
verbatim: A financial institution must establish appropriate procedures to independently review and approve system changes. The financial institution must also establish and test contingency plans in the event of an unsuccessful implementation of material changes to minimise any business disruption.
translation: A financial institution must establish procedures to independently review and approve system changes. The institution must also establish and test contingency plans to handle unsuccessful implementations of material changes so that business disruption is minimised.
evidence: Change Management Policy and Independent Review Procedures
Change Advisory Board (CAB) Terms of Reference and Membership Records
System Change Records with Independent Approval Evidence
Material Change Contingency and Rollback Plans
Rollback and Contingency Plan Test Records

artifacts.description: Mandatory document for Change Management Procedure

artifacts.name: Change Request Records
artifacts.description: Mandatory document for Change Request Records

artifacts.name: Post-Implementation Review Report
artifacts.description: Review report assessing project outcomes and lessons learned

title: Third Party Service Provider System Changes
verbatim:
In relation to critical systems that are developed or maintained by third party service provider, a financial institution must, through contractual obligations, require third party service provider to:
(a) provide sufficient notice to the financial institution before any changes are undertaken that may impact the IT system;
(b) demonstrate that it adopts secure by design principles in IT system development methodology to mitigate cyber risks from propagating across the supply chain; and
(c) ensure the source code continues to be readily accessible for business continuity.
translation: Where critical systems are developed or maintained by a third party service provider, the financial institution must use contractual obligations to require the provider to give sufficient advance notice before making changes that could impact the IT system, demonstrate that it applies secure-by-design principles in its development methodology, and ensure that the source code remains readily accessible for business continuity purposes.
evidence: Third Party Contracts with Change Notification Obligations
TPSP Change Notification Log and Institution Response Records
TPSP Secure-by-Design Attestation and SDLC Evidence
Source Code Escrow or Access Arrangement Agreement
Critical System Register with TPSP Classification

artifacts.name: Emergency Change Procedure
artifacts.description: Procedure for handling emergency changes including retrospective review requirements

artifacts.name: Emergency Change Records
artifacts.description: Records of all emergency changes with retrospective review and approval

title: Critical System Decommissioning
verbatim: When decommissioning critical systems, a financial institution must ensure minimal adverse impact on customers and business operations. This includes establishing and testing contingency plans in the event of unsuccessful system decommissioning.
translation: When decommissioning critical systems, a financial institution must ensure that the process has minimal adverse impact on customers and business operations. This includes establishing and testing contingency plans to manage situations where the decommissioning is unsuccessful.
evidence: Critical System Decommissioning Policy and Procedure
Critical System Inventory and Decommissioning Scope Register
Decommissioning Project Plan and Impact Assessment

title: Automated Development and Security Tools
verbatim: A financial institution may deploy automated tools for software development, testing, software deployment, change management, code scanning and software version control to facilitate timely security assessment of critical systems in keeping with growing complexity in IT systems and emerging cyber threats.
translation: A financial institution may deploy automated tools for software development, testing, deployment, change management, code scanning, and software version control. These tools facilitate timely security assessment of critical systems in response to growing IT complexity and emerging cyber threats.
evidence: Automated Tooling Inventory and Governance Register
CI/CD Pipeline Configuration and Execution Logs for Critical Systems
Code Scanning Results and Remediation Tracking Records

title: Third Party Software Supply Chain Risk
verbatim:
Where a third party software is used, a financial institution should consider the potential risks and impacts a cyber supply chain incident may pose to its overall business operations and services. A financial institution may consider-
(a) adopting Software-Bill-of-Materials (SBOM) to automate the identification and continuous monitoring of potential security vulnerabilities including security issues associated with third party software components; and
(b) establishing open-source software security policy and procedures. This includes ensuring secure access to source code repositories in third party platforms, regular monitoring to prevent data leakages, adoption of secure coding practices, robust testing of open-source software and timely vulnerability assessment to mitigate security vulnerabilities and propagation of malwares across supply chain.
translation: Where third-party software is used, a financial institution should assess the potential risks and impacts a cyber supply chain incident could have on its business operations and services. The institution may consider adopting a Software Bill of Materials to automate identification and continuous monitoring of security vulnerabilities in third-party components, and establishing an open-source software security policy that includes secure access to repositories, monitoring for data leakages, secure coding practices, robust testing, and timely vulnerability assessment.
evidence: Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) Policy and Procedure
SBOM Inventory and Vulnerability Monitoring Records
Open-Source Software (OSS) Security Policy and Procedures
Source Code Repository Access Control Evidence
Supply Chain Incident and Data Leakage Monitoring Records

artifacts.name: SBOM Policy
artifacts.description: Mandatory document for SBOM Policy

artifacts.name: Open Source Security Policy
artifacts.description: Mandatory document for Open Source Security Policy

title: Shadow IT Risk Management
verbatim: A financial institution must develop and implement robust policies to identify and reduce shadow IT risks.
translation: A financial institution must develop and implement robust policies to identify and reduce shadow IT risks.

title: Vulnerability and End-of-Life System Management
translation: A financial institution must ensure that all systems and digital services do not run with known security vulnerabilities, on outdated platforms, or on end-of-life technology. To achieve this, the institution must maintain a current security baseline, continuously monitor and apply the latest patches in a timely manner, plan and implement remedial action for systems approaching end-of-life, and obtain management approval for any exception allowing continued use of unsupported technology, backed by a thorough risk assessment with a clear phase-out timeline that is reviewed at least annually.
evidence: Technology Security Baseline Standards
Patch Management Register and SLA Compliance Report
Vulnerability Scanning Results and Remediation Evidence
EOL Technology Register and Remediation Plan
EOL Exception Records with Risk Assessment and Management Approval

artifacts.description: Formal policy defining patch identification, evaluation, testing, and deployment processes

artifacts.name: Vulnerability Management Procedure
artifacts.description: Mandatory document for Vulnerability Management Procedure

title: Patch and EOL Management Framework
translation: A financial institution must establish a patch and end-of-life management framework that covers: identification and risk assessment of all technology assets for patch or EOL vulnerabilities; criteria, priority, and turnaround times for patch deployment based on vulnerability severity; compatibility testing before patches are deployed; adherence to an end-to-end workflow for patch deployment including approval, testing, monitoring, and tracking; and end-user awareness to support orderly transition.
evidence: Technology Asset Inventory with Patch and EOL Risk Register
Patch Management Policy and Prioritisation Framework
Patch Compatibility Testing Records
End-to-End Patch Deployment Workflow Records and Approval Logs
EOL System Register, Risk Acceptance, and Migration Roadmap

title: Technology Effectiveness Monitoring
verbatim:
A financial institution must continually monitor the effectiveness and security of the technology in use, incorporating developments in technology that may disrupt existing security controls. A financial institution shall-
(a) ensure its board receives advice on the potential impact to business operation arising from evolving technology landscape;
(b) formulate long-term strategy to address anticipated changes with allocation of competent resources to manage associated risks, including new cyber adversary tactics and techniques; and
(c) establish roadmap for system migration to preserve security and reliability of the technology infrastructure in an orderly manner.
translation: A financial institution must continually monitor the effectiveness and security of its technology in use, taking into account developments that may disrupt existing security controls. The institution must ensure the board receives advice on the impact of evolving technology, formulate a long-term strategy with competent resources to manage associated risks including new cyber adversary tactics, and establish a roadmap for system migration to preserve security and reliability in an orderly manner.
evidence: Board Technology Effectiveness Advisory Reports
Long-Term Technology Risk and Cyber Strategy
System Migration Roadmap
Technology Effectiveness Monitoring Programme
Competent Resource Register and Skills Assurance Evidence

title: Cryptography Policy and Standards
translation: A financial institution must establish a robust cryptography policy that promotes strong cryptographic controls for protecting important data and information. At a minimum, the policy must address adoption of industry-standard algorithms and protocols, secure management of cryptographic key lifecycles, at least annual review of cryptographic standards for critical and customer-facing systems, expansion of the IT asset inventory to include all cryptographic tools and algorithms, and the development and testing of compromise-recovery plans in the event of a cryptographic key compromise.
evidence: Cryptography Policy Document
Cryptographic Key Lifecycle Management Procedures
Annual Cryptographic Standards Review Records
Cryptographic Asset Inventory
Cryptographic Key Compromise Recovery Plan and Test Records

artifacts.description: Mandatory document for Cryptography Policy

artifacts.description: Mandatory document for Key Management Procedure

title: Cryptographic Controls Due Diligence
translation: A financial institution must conduct due diligence to evaluate the cryptographic controls of the technology it uses to protect confidentiality, integrity, authentication, authorisation, and non-repudiation of information. Except for non-critical systems without customer information, the institution must retain ownership and control of its encryption keys; where it does not generate its own keys, it must ensure robust controls and industry-standard processes are in place; and it must give due regard to system resources required for cryptographic controls and the risk of reduced network traffic visibility from encryption.
evidence: Cryptographic Controls Due Diligence Assessment
Encryption Key Ownership and Control Register
Third-Party Key Generation Controls Assessment
Third-Party Assessment Reliance Risk Evaluation
Cryptographic Performance and Capacity Impact Assessment

artifacts.description: Framework governing the full lifecycle of cryptographic keys

artifacts.description: Evidence of HSM deployment and configuration for critical system keys

artifacts.name: Key Lifecycle Records
artifacts.description: Records of key generation, rotation, revocation, and destruction activities

title: Cryptographic Protocol Implementation
verbatim: A financial institution must ensure cryptographic controls are based on the effective implementation of suitable cryptographic protocols. The protocols shall include secret and public cryptographic key protocols, both of which shall reflect a high degree of protection to the applicable secret or private cryptographic keys. The selection of such protocols must be based on recognised international standards and tested accordingly. Commensurate with the level of risk, secret cryptographic key and private-cryptographic key storage and encryption / decryption computation must be undertaken in a protected environment, supported by a hardware security module (HSM), trusted execution environment (TEE) or similarly secured devices.
translation: A financial institution must ensure its cryptographic controls are based on the effective implementation of suitable cryptographic protocols, including both secret and public key protocols that reflect a high degree of protection for secret and private keys. Protocol selection must be based on recognised international standards and must be tested accordingly, and the storage and computation of secret and private keys must be undertaken in a protected environment supported by a hardware security module, trusted execution environment, or similarly secured device, commensurate with the level of risk.
evidence: Cryptographic Protocol Standards and Selection Register
Cryptographic Protocol Testing and Validation Records
Key Management Policy and Procedures
HSM / TEE Inventory and Configuration Evidence
Cryptographic Risk Assessment and Commensurate Control Mapping

artifacts.name: Encryption Standards
artifacts.description: Standards specifying minimum encryption requirements for data at rest and in transit

artifacts.name: Encryption Implementation Evidence
artifacts.description: Evidence of encryption deployment across systems and data stores

artifacts.name: Cryptographic Algorithm Inventory
artifacts.description: Inventory of all cryptographic algorithms in use with assessment of adequacy

title: Public Key Certificate Management
verbatim: A financial institution shall store public cryptographic keys in a certificate issued by a Certificate Authority, as appropriate to the level of risk. Such certificates associated with customers shall be issued by recognised Certificate Authorities. The financial institution must ensure that the implementation of authentication and signature protocols using such certificates are subject to strong protection to ensure that the use of private cryptographic keys corresponding to the user certificates are legally binding and irrefutable. The initial issuance and subsequent renewal of such certificates must be consistent with industry best practices and applicable legal / regulatory specifications.
translation: A financial institution must store public cryptographic keys in certificates issued by a Certificate Authority appropriate to the risk level, and certificates associated with customers must be issued by recognised Certificate Authorities. The institution must ensure that authentication and signature protocols using these certificates are strongly protected so that the use of corresponding private keys is legally binding and irrefutable, and the initial issuance and renewal of certificates must be consistent with industry best practices and applicable legal and regulatory requirements.
evidence: PKI Governance Policy and Certificate Management Framework
Certificate Inventory and Lifecycle Management Register
Recognised CA Validation Records for Customer Certificates
Authentication and Digital Signature Protocol Implementation Documentation
Certificate Issuance and Renewal Standard Operating Procedures with Compliance Evidence

title: Data Centre Redundancy
verbatim: A financial institution must ensure data centres have redundant capacity components and multiple distribution paths serving the computer equipment to eliminate any single point of failure for effective achievement of the identified business recovery objectives.
translation: A financial institution must ensure its data centres have redundant capacity components and multiple distribution paths serving the computer equipment, so as to eliminate any single point of failure and effectively achieve the institution's identified business recovery objectives.

title: Production Data Centre Physical Security
verbatim: A financial institution shall host critical systems in a dedicated space intended for production data centre usage. The dedicated space must be physically secured from unauthorised access and is not located in a disaster-prone area. A financial institution must also ensure there is no single point of failure in the design and connectivity for critical components of the production data centres, including hardware components, electrical utility, thermal management and data centre infrastructure. A financial institution must also ensure adequate maintenance, and holistic and continuous monitoring of these critical components with timely alerts on faults and indicators of potential issues.
translation: A financial institution must host critical systems in a dedicated production data centre space that is physically secured from unauthorised access and is not located in a disaster-prone area. The institution must also ensure there is no single point of failure in the design and connectivity of critical data centre components including hardware, electrical utility, thermal management, and infrastructure, and must ensure adequate maintenance and continuous monitoring of these components with timely alerts on faults and potential issues.
evidence: Data Centre Site Risk and Location Assessment
Physical Access Control Policy and Access Logs
Redundancy Architecture Documentation — Power, Cooling, and Network
Preventive Maintenance Schedule and Completion Records
Continuous Monitoring and Alerting Configuration with Alert Response Records

title: Data Centre Operations Control Procedures
verbatim: A financial institution must establish adequate control procedures for its data centre operations, including the deployment of relevant automated tools for batch processing management to ensure timely and accurate batch processes. These control procedures shall also include procedures for implementing changes in the production system, error handling as well as management of other exceptional conditions.
translation: A financial institution must establish adequate control procedures for its data centre operations, including deployment of relevant automated tools for batch processing management to ensure timely and accurate batch processes. These procedures must also cover implementing changes in the production system, error handling, and management of other exceptional conditions.

title: Data Centre Activity Segregation
verbatim: A financial institution must segregate incompatible activities in the data centre operations environment to prevent any unauthorised activity. In the case where vendors' or programmers' access to the production environment is necessary, these activities must be properly authorised and monitored.
translation: A financial institution must segregate incompatible activities in its data centre operations environment to prevent unauthorised activity. Where vendor or programmer access to the production environment is necessary, such access must be properly authorised and monitored.

title: System Capacity Planning
verbatim: A financial institution must ensure its system capacity needs are well-planned and managed with due regard to peak processing period, business growth plans and technology architecture changes.
translation: A financial institution must ensure its system capacity needs are well-planned and managed. Planning must take into account peak processing periods, business growth plans, and changes to technology architecture.

title: Real-Time Performance Monitoring
verbatim: A financial institution must establish real-time monitoring mechanisms to track capacity utilisation and performance of key processes and services. These monitoring mechanisms shall be capable of providing actionable alerts to administrators to enable timely detection and resolution of service interruptions. The monitoring scope, metrics and thresholds shall be updated periodically to ensure they remain effective.
translation: A financial institution must establish real-time monitoring mechanisms that track capacity utilisation and performance of key processes and services. These mechanisms must be capable of generating actionable alerts to enable administrators to detect and resolve service interruptions in a timely manner. The monitoring scope, metrics, and thresholds must be reviewed and updated periodically to remain effective.
evidence: Real-Time Monitoring Architecture and Coverage Register
Monitoring Dashboard Screenshots and Live System Evidence
Alert Configuration and Threshold Documentation
Alert Response and Incident Correlation Records
Periodic Monitoring Review Records

title: Digital Services Resilience Enhancement
verbatim:
A financial institution shall enhance the resilience of key digital services and delivery channels through the following measures:
(a) implement effective mechanisms by 30 September 2027 to provide early signals or warning of service degradation or intermittent failures. This means the capability to:
(i) detect failed transactions and measure service availability more accurately;
(ii) monitor the number of affected customers and transaction volumes during service disruptions to measure the extent and severity customer impact; and
(iii) immediately escalating to senior management when service disruptions affect 5% or more of expected daily customers or transaction volumes;
(b) conduct regular reviews to identify and mitigate potentially vulnerable IT system interdependencies to prevent simultaneous interruptions to multiple digital services or delivery channels; and
(c) establish stand-in processing arrangement by 30 September 2027 and deploy such arrangement during disruptions to ensure continuity of services during disruptions. A financial institution shall prioritise deployment of this capability for digital services and delivery channels which are least substitutable, and ensure its customers are well informed on the terms of use (such as the transaction limit), associated risks and allocation of responsibilities between parties and mitigation actions against fraud risks.
translation: A financial institution must enhance the resilience of key digital services and delivery channels through several measures. By 30 September 2027, it must implement mechanisms that detect failed transactions, measure service availability, monitor the number of affected customers and transaction volumes during disruptions, and immediately escalate to senior management when disruptions affect 5% or more of expected daily customers or transaction volumes. It must also conduct regular reviews to identify and mitigate vulnerable IT system interdependencies, and establish stand-in processing arrangements by 30 September 2027 to maintain continuity during disruptions, prioritising services that are least substitutable and ensuring customers are informed of terms of use, risks, responsibilities, and fraud mitigation measures.
evidence: Early Warning Monitoring System — Design and Capability Documentation
5% Threshold Escalation Policy and Incident Escalation Records
IT System Interdependency Review Records
Stand-In Processing Arrangement — Design, Policy, and Deployment Records
Substitutability Assessment for Digital Services Prioritisation

artifacts.name: Disaster Recovery Plans
artifacts.description: DR plans for all critical systems with recovery procedures and roles

artifacts.name: DR Roles and Responsibilities Matrix
artifacts.description: Matrix defining DR roles, responsibilities, and escalation paths

artifacts.name: DR Communication Plan
artifacts.description: Communication plan for use during disaster recovery activation

title: Critical System High Availability Requirements
verbatim: For critical systems, where there is a reasonable expectation for immediate delivery of service to customers or dealings with counterparties, a financial institution must ensure that these systems are designed for high availability with a cumulative unplanned downtime affecting the interface with customers or counterparties of not more than 4 hours on a rolling 12 months basis and a maximum tolerable downtime of 120 minutes per incident.
translation: For critical systems where customers or counterparties have a reasonable expectation of immediate service delivery, a financial institution must design those systems for high availability. The cumulative unplanned downtime affecting the interface with customers or counterparties must not exceed 4 hours on a rolling 12-month basis, and the maximum tolerable downtime per incident must not exceed 120 minutes.
evidence: Critical System Register with HA Classification
High Availability Architecture Design Documentation
Rolling 12-Month Unplanned Downtime Tracking Report
Incident Records with Downtime Classification and Duration
High Availability Test and Failover Validation Records

artifacts.name: DR Test Results Report
artifacts.description: Report on disaster recovery testing including RTO/RPO validation

artifacts.name: Failover Test Records
artifacts.description: Records of full failover tests to the disaster recovery site

artifacts.name: DR Deficiency Remediation Plan
artifacts.description: Plan for remediating deficiencies identified during DR testing

title: Non-NCII Entity Service Availability Guidance
verbatim: Eligible e-money issuers, non-bank registered merchant acquirers and intermediary remittance institutions that have not been designated as a National Critical Information Infrastructure (NCII) entity are encouraged to implement the measures provided in paragraph 10.31.
translation: Eligible e-money issuers, non-bank registered merchant acquirers, and intermediary remittance institutions that have not been designated as National Critical Information Infrastructure (NCII) entities are encouraged to implement the resilience measures set out in paragraph 10.31. This is guidance rather than a mandatory requirement for those entities.
evidence: NCII Designation Status Confirmation
Paragraph 10.31 Voluntary Adoption Assessment
Service Availability Policy and Target Documentation
Board and Senior Management Awareness Record
Compensating Controls Register (Where Paragraph 10.31 Measures Not Fully Adopted)

artifacts.name: RTO/RPO Definitions
artifacts.description: Documented RTO and RPO targets for all critical systems with business owner approval

artifacts.description: Mapping between business impact analysis results and RTO/RPO targets

title: Technology Diversity for Resilience
verbatim: A financial institution shall prioritise diversity in technology to enhance resilience by ensuring critical systems infrastructure are not excessively exposed to similar technology risks.
translation: A financial institution must prioritise diversity in technology to enhance resilience. Specifically, it must ensure that critical systems infrastructure is not excessively exposed to similar technology risks.
evidence: Technology Diversity Policy and Framework
Critical Systems Inventory with Technology Stack Taxonomy
Technology Concentration Risk Assessment
Technology Procurement and Architecture Review Standards
Resilience Testing Evidence Demonstrating Diversity Effectiveness

artifacts.name: Business Impact Analysis Report
artifacts.description: Annual BIA identifying critical processes and their technology dependencies

artifacts.name: Technology Dependency Mapping
artifacts.description: Mapping of critical business processes to supporting technology services

artifacts.name: BIA Review Records
artifacts.description: Annual review and update records for the business impact analysis

title: Service Interruption Response
translation: During any interruption, performance degradation, or intermittent failure of digital services or delivery channels, a financial institution must respond promptly and effectively to minimise customer impact. This includes ensuring timely escalation and decision-making to resume services within the timeframe in paragraph 10.32, defining clear accountabilities and formalising arrangements with third party service providers for recovery, establishing a communication plan to inform affected customers and provide frequent updates and actionable alternatives, giving customers a convenient means to check service availability, and disclosing the quarterly track record of service availability within 15 calendar days of quarter-end beginning from 15 October 2027.
evidence: Incident Escalation and Response Procedure
Incident Response Drill and Test Records
Third-Party Service Provider Recovery Arrangements
Service Interruption Customer Communication Plan
Real-Time Digital Service Availability Status Page

artifacts.description: Plan defining communication protocols during technology incidents and disruptions

artifacts.description: Maintained contact lists for crisis notification of stakeholders

artifacts.description: Records of crisis communication testing during continuity exercises

title: Enterprise Network Design
verbatim: A financial institution must design and implement a reliable, scalable and secure enterprise network that is able to support its business activities, including future growth plans.
translation: A financial institution must design and implement an enterprise network that is reliable, scalable, and secure. The network must be capable of supporting the institution's current business activities as well as its future growth plans.
evidence: Enterprise Network Architecture Document
Network Capacity Planning and Scalability Assessment
Network Security Architecture and Controls Documentation
Network Reliability and Redundancy Design Evidence
Network Change Management Records

artifacts.description: Mandatory document for Network Security Policy

artifacts.description: Mandatory document for Network Diagram

title: Network Service Reliability
verbatim: A financial institution must ensure the network services for its critical systems are reliable and have no single point of failure in order to protect the critical systems against potential network faults and cyber threats.
translation: A financial institution must ensure that network services supporting its critical systems are reliable and contain no single point of failure. This is required to protect critical systems against potential network faults and cyber threats.
evidence: Critical System Network Architecture Diagrams
Critical System Inventory with Network Dependency Mapping
Network Redundancy and Failover Test Records

Network Incident Records for Critical System Outages

title: Network Fault Prevention Measures
verbatim: The control measures to prevent from network faults as referred to in paragraph 10.37 are expected to include component redundancy, service diversity and alternate network paths.
translation: The control measures expected to prevent network faults, as referred to in paragraph 10.37, include component redundancy, service diversity, and alternate network paths.
evidence: Network Architecture Diagram — Redundancy and Alternate Paths

Service Diversity Agreements — ISP and Telco Contracts

title: Network Bandwidth Monitoring
verbatim: A financial institution must establish real-time network bandwidth monitoring processes and corresponding network service resilience metrics to flag any over utilisation of bandwidth and system disruptions due to bandwidth congestion and network faults. This includes traffic analysis to detect trends and anomalies.
translation: A financial institution must establish real-time network bandwidth monitoring processes and corresponding resilience metrics to flag over-utilisation of bandwidth and disruptions caused by bandwidth congestion and network faults. Monitoring must include traffic analysis to detect trends and anomalies.
evidence: Real-Time Network Bandwidth Monitoring Policy and Procedures
Network Service Resilience Metrics Register
Real-Time Bandwidth Monitoring Dashboard and Alert Configuration

Network Traffic Trend Analysis and Anomaly Detection Reports

artifacts.description: Records of IDS/IPS alert monitoring, triage, and investigation

title: Network Security for Critical Systems
verbatim: A financial institution must ensure network services supporting critical systems are designed and implemented to ensure the confidentiality, integrity and availability of data.
translation: A financial institution must ensure that network services supporting critical systems are designed and implemented to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of data.
evidence: Network Architecture and Segmentation Design Document
Firewall and Access Control Rule Documentation
Encryption in Transit Policy and Implementation Evidence
Network Redundancy and Availability Design Evidence
Network Security Assessment Reports (Penetration Test and Vulnerability Scan)

title: Network Design Blueprint
verbatim: A financial institution must establish and maintain a network design blueprint identifying all of its internal and external network interfaces and connectivity. The blueprint must highlight both physical and logical connectivity between network components and network segmentations.
translation: A financial institution must establish and maintain a network design blueprint that identifies all internal and external network interfaces and connectivity. The blueprint must highlight both the physical and logical connectivity between network components as well as network segmentations.
evidence: Master Network Design Blueprint
Physical Network Topology Diagrams
Logical Network Topology Diagrams

title: Network Device Log Retention
verbatim: A financial institution must ensure sufficient and relevant network device logs are retained for investigations and forensic purposes for at least three years.
translation: A financial institution must retain sufficient and relevant network device logs to support investigations and forensic purposes. These logs must be kept for at least three years.
evidence: Network Device Log Retention Policy
Network Device Log Source Inventory
SIEM / Log Management Platform Retention Configuration Evidence
Log Integrity and Tamper-Evidence Controls Documentation

title: Network Segmentation Safeguards
verbatim: A financial institution must implement appropriate safeguards to minimise the risk of a system compromise in one entity affecting other entities within the group. Safeguards implemented may include establishing logical network segmentation for the financial institution from other entities within the group.
translation: A financial institution must implement appropriate safeguards to minimise the risk that a system compromise in one entity within the group affects other entities. Safeguards may include establishing logical network segmentation between the financial institution and other entities within the group.
evidence: Group Network Architecture Diagram
Firewall Rule Sets and Access Control Lists at Group Boundaries
Network Segmentation Policy and Group-Level Interconnection Standards
Network Segmentation Testing and Validation Records
Inter-Entity Connectivity Register and Approval Records

artifacts.description: Architecture and tooling documentation for comprehensive network monitoring

artifacts.description: Procedures for 24/7 network monitoring and anomaly investigation

title: Backup Strategy and Procedures
translation: A financial institution must establish a robust backup strategy and procedures to meet business recovery objectives. At a minimum, this requires establishing backup and restoration procedures to manage the backup data life cycle, maintaining adequate backup copies of all critical data, software, and logs, storing backup media in an environmentally secure and access-controlled site, securing the storage and transportation of sensitive data in removable media, periodically testing backup and restoration procedures and promptly fixing the root causes of any failures, and conducting an independent risk assessment of the end-to-end backup storage and delivery management to ensure controls adequately protect sensitive data at all times.
evidence: Backup Strategy Policy and Procedures
Critical Systems and Backup Coverage Register
Backup Site Security Controls Evidence
Removable Media Security Controls Evidence
Backup and Restoration Testing Records

artifacts.description: Policy defining backup scope, frequency, retention, and storage requirements

artifacts.name: Backup and Restoration Procedure
artifacts.description: Mandatory document for Backup and Restoration Procedure

artifacts.name: Backup Testing Records
artifacts.description: Mandatory document for Backup Testing Records

title: Tamper-Proof Backup and Isolated Recovery
verbatim: A financial institution shall establish a tamper-proof backup arrangement and an isolated recovery environment to enable timely resumption of critical banking and payment services within its tolerable level in the event of destructive cyber-attacks such as widespread data loss caused by ransomware.
translation: A financial institution must establish a tamper-proof backup arrangement and an isolated recovery environment. These measures must enable timely resumption of critical banking and payment services within the institution's tolerable level in the event of destructive cyber-attacks such as widespread data loss caused by ransomware.
evidence: Tamper-Proof Backup Policy and Architecture Documentation
Backup Immutability Technical Evidence and Audit Logs
Isolated Recovery Environment Design and Configuration Records
Ransomware Recovery Testing Records and Results
Tolerable Downtime and Recovery Objective Alignment Records

artifacts.description: Procedure for regular backup restoration testing

artifacts.name: Test Failure Remediation Records
artifacts.description: Records of remediation actions for backup restoration test failures

title: Third Party Service Provider Oversight
verbatim: The board and senior management of the financial institution must exercise effective oversight and address associated risks when engaging third party service providers for critical technology functions and systems. The financial institution remains accountable for managing all risks that arise from engagement of third party service providers, to ensure security and reliability of technology services in compliance with all relevant regulatory requirements prescribed in this policy document.
translation: The board and senior management must exercise effective oversight and address associated risks when the institution engages third party service providers for critical technology functions and systems. The financial institution remains fully accountable for managing all risks arising from such engagements, ensuring that security and reliability of technology services comply with all relevant regulatory requirements in this policy document.
evidence: Board and Senior Management TPSP Oversight Framework
Critical TPSP Register and Classification Evidence
TPSP Risk Assessments for Critical Technology Functions
Critical TPSP Contractual Agreements with Regulatory Compliance Obligations
Ongoing TPSP Performance Monitoring and Board Reporting Records

artifacts.name: Third Party Risk Management Framework
artifacts.description: Mandatory document for Third Party Risk Management Framework

artifacts.name: Vendor Inventory
artifacts.description: Mandatory document for Vendor Inventory

title: Third Party Due Diligence
verbatim: A financial institution must conduct a due diligence on a third party service provider prior to its on-boarding or engagement and throughout the service engagement to ensure achievement of business performance and recovery objectives remain unimpaired, considering the latest risk environment. At the minimum, a financial institution must consider the range of risks outlined in Appendix 8.
translation: A financial institution must conduct due diligence on a third party service provider before on-boarding or engaging it, and must continue that due diligence throughout the service engagement. The due diligence must ensure that business performance and recovery objectives remain unimpaired in light of the latest risk environment, and must at a minimum consider the range of risks outlined in Appendix 8.
evidence: Third Party Due Diligence Policy and Framework
Pre-Engagement Due Diligence Assessment Records
Ongoing Due Diligence Review Schedule and Completed Review Records
Appendix 8 Risk Coverage Matrix
Third Party Risk Register with Current Risk Environment Assessment

artifacts.name: Outsourcing Governance Framework
artifacts.description: Framework defining roles, responsibilities, and oversight for technology outsourcing

artifacts.name: Board Outsourcing Approvals
artifacts.description: Board approval records for material outsourcing arrangements

artifacts.name: Outsourcing Monitoring Reports
artifacts.description: Reports on oversight and monitoring of outsourced technology activities

title: Service Level Agreement Requirements
translation: A financial institution must establish a Service Level Agreement (SLA) when engaging a third party service provider. At a minimum, the SLA must include: regulator access rights to records, files, and data; sufficient prior notice requirements for substantial sub-contracting; a written undertaking on compliance with secrecy provisions that survives contract termination; disaster recovery and backup arrangements where applicable; service level objectives for uptime or availability; business continuity arrangements on exit or termination including timely data recovery; the third party's obligation to promptly disclose service disruptions or cyber incidents affecting the institution or its customer data; requirements for the third party to comply with relevant internationally recognised standards and maintain up-to-date staff certifications; and requirements for the third party to participate in the institution's security awareness and education programme where appropriate.
evidence: Third Party SLA Master Register and Executed Agreements
Regulator and Auditor Access Rights Provisions and Exercise Records
Sub-contracting Notification Framework and Confidentiality Undertaking Records
Disaster Recovery, Backup, Exit and Business Continuity Arrangements
Service Level Objectives Monitoring and Performance Reporting Records

artifacts.name: SLA Management Procedure
artifacts.description: Mandatory document for SLA Management Procedure

artifacts.name: Service Level Agreements
artifacts.description: Documented SLAs for all critical technology services

title: Continuous Third Party Cybersecurity Monitoring
translation: A financial institution must formulate a roadmap to achieve continuous monitoring of a third party service provider's cybersecurity posture to obtain real-time insights for effective incident management. This requires measuring and regularly managing the IT infrastructure footprint and customer information accessible to third parties, adopting leading security policies for third party risks, incorporating third party protocols into incident response plans, defining a priority set of controls requiring more frequent assurance assertions, monitoring third party disclosures of technology and cyber incidents at higher frequency, implementing technology solutions to automate metric testing, and establishing processes to respond to breached thresholds including investigating failed assertions and remedying control gaps.
evidence: Third Party Continuous Monitoring Roadmap
Third Party IT Footprint and Customer Data Exposure Register
Third Party Security Control Framework and Product-Specific Risk Policy
Incident Response Plan — Third Party Protocols and Software Update Provisions
Continuous Monitoring Technology Platform Evidence and Automated Metric Testing Records

artifacts.name: Vendor Exit Strategy
artifacts.description: Documented exit strategies for all critical technology vendors

artifacts.name: Data Migration Plan
artifacts.description: Plan for migrating data and transitioning services from vendor

artifacts.name: Exit Plan Review Records
artifacts.description: Annual review or testing records for vendor exit strategies

title: Cloud Services Risk Assessment
translation: A financial institution must fully understand the inherent risks of adopting cloud services and must conduct a comprehensive risk assessment prior to cloud adoption. The assessment must address risks arising from the cloud's shared resource and multi-tenant architecture, and must specifically cover the sophistication of the deployment model, migration risks, the geographic location of cloud infrastructure including geo-political and legal risks, multi-tenancy and data co-mingling, vendor lock-in and portability, ability to customise security configurations, exposure to cyber-attacks via cloud providers, termination of the cloud provider including data recovery, demarcation of responsibilities and liability, and the ability to meet regulatory requirements and international standards on a continuing basis.
evidence: Pre-Adoption Cloud Risk Assessment Report
Cloud Architecture and Deployment Model Documentation
Cloud Migration Risk Assessment and Data Residency Analysis
Multi-Tenancy Controls Assessment and Cyber Risk Exposure Analysis
Vendor Lock-in Assessment and Cloud Exit Strategy

artifacts.name: Cloud Risk Assessment Methodology
artifacts.description: Methodology for assessing risks of cloud service adoption

artifacts.name: Cloud Risk Assessment Reports
artifacts.description: Completed risk assessments for all cloud service adoptions

title: Public Cloud Key Risks and Controls
verbatim: For critical systems hosted on a public cloud, a financial institution is expected to consider common key risks and control measures as specified in Appendix 10. A financial institution that relies on alternative risk management practices that depart from the measures outlined in Appendix 10 is expected to be prepared to explain and demonstrate to the Bank that these alternative practices are at least as effective as, or superior to, the measures in Appendix 10.
translation: For critical systems hosted on a public cloud, a financial institution is expected to consider the common key risks and control measures specified in Appendix 10. A financial institution that departs from those measures with alternative risk management practices must be prepared to explain and demonstrate to the Bank that those alternative practices are at least as effective as, or superior to, the measures in Appendix 10.
evidence: Critical Systems Register — Public Cloud Footprint
Appendix 10 Risk and Control Mapping Matrix
Cloud Risk Register — Appendix 10 Aligned
Alternative Control Justification and Equivalence Assessment
Cloud Governance Framework and Board-Level Oversight Records

artifacts.name: Access Control Policy
artifacts.description: Formal access control policy defining rules and rights for all systems and assets

artifacts.name: Annual Policy Review Records
artifacts.description: Records of annual review and approval of the access control policy

artifacts.name: Policy Communication Records
artifacts.description: Evidence of access control policy communication to all staff

title: Cloud Data Protection Safeguards
verbatim: A financial institution must implement appropriate safeguards on customer and counterparty information and proprietary data when using cloud services to protect against unauthorised disclosure and access. This shall include retaining ownership, control and management of all data pertaining to customer and counterparty information, proprietary data and services hosted on the cloud, including the relevant cryptographic keys management.
translation: A financial institution must implement appropriate safeguards to protect customer and counterparty information and proprietary data when using cloud services against unauthorised disclosure and access. This includes retaining ownership, control, and management of all such data and services hosted on the cloud, including the management of the relevant cryptographic keys.
evidence: Cloud Data Protection Policy and Framework
Cloud-Hosted Data Inventory and Classification Register
Cryptographic Key Management Framework and Key Inventory
Cloud Identity and Access Management (IAM) Controls Evidence
Data Residency and Sovereignty Controls Documentation

artifacts.name: Data Residency Agreements
artifacts.description: Agreements with cloud providers confirming data residency commitments

title: Access Control Policy
verbatim: A financial institution must implement an access control policy for the identification, authentication, and authorisation of all users to its IT assets and data. The level of granularity defined in the access control policy shall be commensurate with the level of risk of unauthorised access to its IT assets.
translation: A financial institution must implement an access control policy covering the identification, authentication, and authorisation of all users to its IT assets and data. The level of granularity in that policy must be commensurate with the level of risk posed by unauthorised access to those IT assets.
evidence: Access Control Policy Document
IT Asset Risk Classification Register
User Provisioning and Access Request Records
Privileged Access Management Controls Evidence
Periodic Access Review and Recertification Records

artifacts.name: Access Control Policy
artifacts.description: Formal access control policy defining rules and rights for all systems and assets

artifacts.name: Privileged Access Management Policy
artifacts.description: Mandatory document for Privileged Access Management Policy

title: Access Control Principles
translation: A financial institution must adhere to the following access control principles: adopt a default deny-all policy requiring all access to be explicitly authorised; employ least-privilege access rights so users receive only the minimum permissions needed for their roles; use time-bound access restricted to a specific period based on the nature of work; enforce segregation of incompatible functions so no single person can independently modify, circumvent, or disable system security features; establish criteria for activities requiring dual-authorisation control; and adopt robust user authorisation and authentication based on asset criticality, including stronger authentication for critical activities and remote access, robust identity verification to prevent impersonation, and unique online credentials linked to a single user.
evidence: Access Control Policy and Deny-All Configuration Records
Role-Based Access Matrix and Least-Privilege Review Records
Time-Bound Access Provisioning and Expiry Records
Segregation of Duties Matrix and Conflict Detection Records
Dual-Authorisation Criteria and Enforcement Records

artifacts.name: MFA Policy
artifacts.description: Policy mandating multi-factor authentication for all privileged access to critical systems

artifacts.name: MFA Configuration Evidence
artifacts.description: Screenshots or audit logs demonstrating MFA configuration and enforcement

artifacts.name: Break-Glass Procedure
artifacts.description: Emergency access procedure with MFA controls for break-glass scenarios

artifacts.name: MFA Compliance Report
artifacts.description: Report on MFA adoption and compliance across critical systems

title: Multi-Factor Authentication
verbatim: A financial institution must employ a multi-factor authentication (MFA) that can defend against social engineering attacks for authenticating user access to critical systems. The MFA must combine two or more of knowledge factors, inherent factors (e.g. biometric characteristics) or possession factors (e.g. security keys, tokens).
translation: A financial institution must employ multi-factor authentication (MFA) capable of defending against social engineering attacks for user access to critical systems. The MFA must combine two or more of the following factor types: knowledge factors, inherent factors (such as biometric characteristics), or possession factors (such as security keys or tokens).
evidence: Critical Systems Inventory with MFA Coverage Mapping
MFA Solution Architecture and Anti-Social-Engineering Assessment
MFA Policy and Procedure Documentation
MFA Enrolment and Configuration Evidence
MFA Exception Register and Waiver Records

title: User Access Matrix
verbatim: A financial institution must establish a user access matrix to outline access rights, user roles or profiles, and the authorising and approving authorities. The access matrix must be periodically reviewed and updated.
translation: A financial institution must establish a user access matrix that outlines access rights, user roles or profiles, and the authorising and approving authorities. The access matrix must be periodically reviewed and updated.
evidence: User Access Matrix Document
Role Definition and Authorisation Register
Periodic Access Matrix Review Records
Access Matrix Update and Change Log

title: Access Monitoring and Logging
verbatim:
A financial institution must ensure-
(a) access controls to enterprise-wide systems are effectively managed and monitored;
(b) anomalies are flagged for prompt investigations to contain any cyber incidents; and
(c) user activities in critical systems are logged for audit and investigations. Activity logs must be maintained for at least three years and regularly reviewed in a timely manner.
translation: A financial institution must ensure that access controls to enterprise-wide systems are effectively managed and monitored, that anomalies are flagged promptly for investigation to contain cyber incidents, and that user activities in critical systems are logged for audit and investigation purposes. Activity logs must be maintained for at least three years and must be regularly reviewed in a timely manner.
evidence: Access Control Monitoring Policy and Procedures
Enterprise Access Monitoring Coverage Matrix
Anomaly Detection and Alert Investigation Records
Critical System Activity Logs with Integrity Controls
Log Retention Schedule and Verification Evidence

artifacts.description: Mandatory document for Logging and Monitoring Standards
